original_abstract: The Arab spring, protest movements in the EU, Russia, Turkey or elsewhere, are often labeled as twitter-revolutions. A crucial role is attributed to the new media, coverage of events abroad and ensuing mutual reactions. With the dissemination of print, revolts in early-modern times faced the challenge of a similar media-revolution. This influenced the very face of the events that could become full-fledged propaganda wars once the insurgents had won access to the printing press. But it also had an impact on revolt-narratives. Governments severely persecuted dissident views in such delicate issues as revolts. Observers abroad had no such divided loyalties and were freer to reflect upon the events. Therefore, the book focuses mainly on representations of revolts across borders.
humanized_abstract: Uprisings such as the Arab spring, and protest movements in the European Union, in Russia or in Turkey, are usually referred to as “twitter revolutions”. The new media, as well as foreign coverage of revolutionary events with the subsequent international reactions have played a major role. With the emergence of print media the new media context in early-modern insurrections was the same as in our times in the context of the “twitter revolutions”. But also the character of the revolt itself was probably affected. When an insurrection won the printing press it at the same time became part of a possible propaganda war. This is however a very secondary aspect of our topic. More important are the representations of insurrections beyond the borders of the country where they had broken out. In contrast to the hostile or ambivalent national responses, such foreign representations were not pre-emptively restricted in the same way as was the case for the first accounts of the rebellious popular movement in the “homeland” — because foreigner commentators were in no way divided in their allegiance to the competing centers of power involved.
